One difficulty follows another for our benefit, until we reach a place where sin and sorrow are permanently removed. Argumentative people misunderstand or misinterpret one another's words; proud people will insist on having their own way or completely refuse to help. The approval of the majority cannot be relied upon; and what can others expect, when “Hosanna to the Son of David” quickly turned into “Crucify him, crucify him?”

Joab brutally murdered Amasa. The more planning there is in a sin, the worse it is. Joab willingly sacrificed both the king’s interests and those of the kingdom for his personal revenge. But one would wonder how a murderer could face someone while pursuing a traitor; and how, under such a burden of guilt, he had courage to enter into danger: his conscience was hardened.

It is right that a place which dares to hide a traitor be attacked; nor will the heart fare better which indulges rebellious desires that does not want Christ to reign over them. A wise woman, by her careful handling, satisfied Joab and yet saved the city. Wisdom is not limited to status or gender; it does not consist of deep knowledge but in understanding how to act as situations arise, so that troubles may be avoided and benefits secured. Much harm would be prevented if arguing parties understood one another. Let both sides be corrected. The only condition for peace is the surrender of the traitor. It is so in God’s dealing with the soul when besieged by conviction and distress; sin is the traitor; the cherished desire is the rebel: part with that, cast away the transgression, and all will be well. There is no peace on any other terms.

Here is the condition of David's court after his restoration. It is good when capable people are appointed to perform public duties; let everyone seek to perform those duties as faithful servants to the Son of David.
